message.content:
Adversaries may execute an Endpoint Denial of Service (DoS) attack by overwhelming an operating system (OS) with excessive demands, leading to resource exhaustion. This technique, known as OS Exhaustion Flood, exploits the OS's self-imposed limits on resource management. Common methods include TCP state-exhaustion attacks like SYN floods and ACK floods. In a SYN flood, numerous SYN packets are sent without completing the TCP handshake, quickly exhausting the OS's capacity to handle new TCP connections. ACK floods involve sending a large number of ACK packets, forcing the OS to search its state table for non-existent connections, thereby consuming significant computational resources and causing system sluggishness or unresponsiveness.

The potential impact includes the inability of the targeted system to provide TCP services, leading to service disruption. Adversaries typically exploit this by leveraging the stateful nature of TCP protocols to overwhelm the OS.

Mitigation strategies include implementing rate limiting, using intrusion detection systems (IDS) to identify and block malicious traffic, and configuring firewalls to drop excessive SYN/ACK packets. Additionally, employing load balancers and redundant systems can help distribute the load and maintain service availability.
